vulnerabilities.title: EIP-712 _RENTAL_ORDER_TYPEHASH Uses abi.encode Instead of abi.encodePacked for Type String Concatenation
vulnerabilities.summary: In _deriveRentalTypehashes, the rentalOrderTypeHash is computed as keccak256(abi.encode(rentalOrderTypeString, hookTypeString, itemTypeString)) instead of keccak256(abi.encodePacked(...)). Per EIP-712, the struct type hash must be the keccak256 of concatenated type strings. abi.encode adds ABI padding between dynamic bytes arguments, producing a completely different hash from the EIP-712 specification. This makes the on-chain type hash incompatible with any standard EIP-712 implementation.

description.desc: rentalOrderTypeHash = keccak256(abi.encode(rentalOrderTypeString, hookTypeString, itemTypeString)) uses abi.encode while rentPayloadTypeHash at lines 351-355 correctly uses abi.encodePacked

vulnerabilities.impact: MEDIUM-HIGH severity code defect. The on-chain protocol functions correctly due to internal consistency (same wrong hash used for creation and verification). However: (1) It violates the EIP-712 standard that the code explicitly claims to implement. (2) It breaks interoperability with any standard EIP-712 tooling for rental order hashes. (3) The inconsistency with rentPayloadTypeHash (which correctly uses encodePacked) in the same function demonstrates this is a bug, not a design decision. (4) Fixing this requires contract redeployment since the type hash is stored as an immutable.

vulnerabilities.title: EIP-712 Hook Hash Does Not keccak256 Dynamic bytes extraData Field
vulnerabilities.summary: _deriveHookHash passes hook.extraData directly to abi.encode instead of keccak256(hook.extraData) as required by EIP-712 for dynamic 'bytes' types. This produces non-standard hashes that deviate from EIP-712 specification, affecting all hash derivations that include hooks (metadata hash, rental order hash, rent payload hash).

description.desc: keccak256(abi.encode(_HOOK_TYPEHASH, hook.target, hook.itemIndex, hook.extraData)) — hook.extraData is bytes and should be keccak256(hook.extraData)

vulnerabilities.impact: This is a confirmed EIP-712 specification violation. Impact: (1) Interoperability failure with any EIP-712 compliant off-chain signer or verification tool. (2) The non-standard encoding means the hash output depends on the full ABI encoding of bytes data (including offset and length) rather than just keccak256 of the content, making the hash computation non-standard. (3) All hash derivations that include hooks are affected: order metadata hash, rental order hash, and rent payload hash. The defect exists at deployment and affects all rental orders that include hooks.

vulnerabilities.title: Pro-Rata Calculation Overflow DoS Prevents Early Stop of PAY Orders
vulnerabilities.summary: In PaymentEscrow._calculatePaymentProRata, the expression `(amount * elapsedTime) * 1000` can overflow for large ERC20 amounts. With Solidity 0.8.x checked arithmetic, this overflow causes a revert, preventing pro-rata settlement and thus blocking early termination of PAY orders. The lender must wait until full expiry to stop the rental.

description.desc: uint256 numerator = (amount * elapsedTime) * 1000; — triple multiplication without overflow protection

vulnerabilities.impact: The impact is a temporary denial of service: lenders in affected PAY orders cannot stop rentals early. Their NFTs remain locked until the rental expires naturally. The financial impact is the opportunity cost of locked NFTs for the full rental duration. Recovery IS possible after expiry via the non-pro-rata settlement path. The attack requires extremely large ERC20 amounts (>~1.15e74 with elapsedTime=1), which limits practical exploitability but does not eliminate the code defect.

vulnerabilities.title: Kernel can be permanently bricked by setting executor/admin to address(0) via executeAction (no zero-address guard)
vulnerabilities.summary: Kernel.executeAction allows Actions.ChangeExecutor and Actions.ChangeAdmin without validating target_ != address(0). If set to zero, no one can execute privileged lifecycle actions or manage roles, effectively freezing governance and potentially preventing module upgrades/fixes.

description.desc: Missing non-zero validation on critical governance addresses

vulnerabilities.impact: MEDIUM-HIGH severity. While exploitation requires a compromised or erroneous executor, the consequences are permanent and protocol-wide: inability to upgrade modules, activate/deactivate policies, manage roles, or migrate the kernel. Active rentals would continue to function, but any bugs or needed changes would be unfixable. The only recovery would be deploying an entirely new protocol instance, which would not help with existing rental state locked in the old modules.

vulnerabilities.title: rentalWallet Omitted from _deriveRentalOrderHash Enables Order Hash Collision and State Corruption
vulnerabilities.summary: The _deriveRentalOrderHash function excludes the rentalWallet field from the EIP-712 encoding despite the type string declaring 'address rentalWallet'. This means RentalOrder structs differing only in rentalWallet produce identical hashes. An attacker calling stopRent can substitute a different rentalWallet in the order struct and still pass the hash existence check in Storage.removeRentals. This causes assets to be reclaimed from the wrong Safe, rentalAsset counters for the wrong wallet to be d

description.desc: abi.encode skips order.rentalWallet: encodes order.renter then order.startTimestamp directly, missing the rentalWallet field between them
Affected function: stopRent
Affected function: removeRentals

vulnerabilities.impact: CRITICAL. (1) Assets can be reclaimed from wrong rental Safes, effectively stealing NFTs from innocent renters. (2) Original rental Safe's rentedAssets counters remain permanently elevated, causing the Guard to permanently block those assets from being transferred — even after the rental should have ended. (3) Payment settlement may distribute funds incorrectly. (4) The storage order hash is deleted, so the original rental cannot be properly stopped later.

vulnerabilities.title: orderType and emittedExtraData Omitted from _deriveOrderMetadataHash Allowing Fulfiller to Alter Order Semantics
vulnerabilities.summary: The _deriveOrderMetadataHash function only encodes rentDuration and hooks into the hash, omitting orderType and emittedExtraData despite both being declared in the OrderMetadata EIP-712 type string. This means the lender's Seaport zone hash does not cryptographically bind the order type. A fulfiller can substitute orderType without invalidating the zone hash check in _isValidOrderMetadata. While current item-type validation in _convertToItems mitigates cross-type conversion for most cases, the m

description.desc: abi.encode(_ORDER_METADATA_TYPEHASH, metadata.rentDuration, keccak256(hookHashes)) — metadata.orderType and keccak256(metadata.emittedExtraData) are absent
Affected function: _isValidOrderMetadata

vulnerabilities.impact: Immediate: Arbitrary event data injection via emittedExtraData manipulation, affecting off-chain indexers and protocol integrations. Medium-term: The orderType is not cryptographically bound, which violates the security model. While _convertToItems currently mitigates orderType swaps, this is defense-in-depth rather than the intended security mechanism. Any future code changes relaxing item type constraints would make orderType swaps directly exploitable, reversing payment flows. The defect also means the EIP-712 encoding is non-compliant, breaking interoperability with standard EIP-712 verification tools.

vulnerabilities.title: Guard Completely Bypassed When Hook Is Active — Rented Assets Transferable
vulnerabilities.summary: In Guard.checkTransaction, when a hook is registered and active for a target contract address, execution flow is forwarded entirely to the hook via _forwardToHook and the _checkTransaction function is NOT called. This means all standard protections (blocking NFT transfers, approvals, module changes, guard removal) are completely delegated to the hook contract. If a hook has any bug, missing check, or doesn't replicate all guard protections, rented assets can be transferred out of the safe. The g

description.desc: if (hook != address(0) && isActive) { _forwardToHook(...); } else { _checkTransaction(...); } — mutually exclusive branches, hook bypasses all standard checks

vulnerabilities.impact: CRITICAL: Any rented NFT whose contract address has an active hook is at risk if the hook doesn't perfectly replicate all guard protections. This includes protection against: ERC721 transfers (transferFrom, safeTransferFrom), ERC721 approvals (approve), ERC1155 transfers, setApprovalForAll, setGuard removal, and module enable/disable. A single missing check in any hook compromises all rentals for assets on that hooked contract. The guard's fundamental purpose—protecting rented assets—is completely undermined for hooked targets.

vulnerabilities.title: Guard Bypass via Non-Standard NFT Function Selectors Not Covered by _checkTransaction
vulnerabilities.summary: Guard._checkTransaction only blocks a hardcoded set of ERC721/ERC1155 function selectors (transferFrom, safeTransferFrom, approve, setApprovalForAll, safeBatchTransferFrom). Any non-standard transfer or approval function (e.g., EIP-4494 permit, custom burn, permit-and-transfer patterns) is NOT blocked. If a rented NFT implements additional transfer mechanisms beyond the standard selectors, the renter can use these to move or approve rented assets out of the safe. The guard's else branch for unkn

description.desc: Hardcoded selector checks only cover 5 ERC721 and 2 ERC1155 selectors plus 3 Safe selectors. All other selectors pass through without blocking.

vulnerabilities.impact: HIGH severity. Any rented NFT that implements additional transfer or approval mechanisms beyond the standard ERC721/ERC1155 selectors can be stolen from the rental safe. This includes NFTs implementing EIP-4494 (permit), custom transfer functions, or any other approval-granting mechanism. The impact is total loss of the lender's NFT. While the precondition requires a non-standard NFT, EIP-4494 adoption is growing and the guard provides a false sense of security.

vulnerabilities.title: Whitelisted Delegate Call Target Can Bypass Guard and Steal All Rented Assets via Batched Internal Calls
vulnerabilities.summary: If a contract like Gnosis MultiSend is whitelisted as a delegate call target (via toggleWhitelistDelegate), a rental safe owner can delegatecall to it and execute arbitrary internal calls that bypass the guard entirely. When a safe delegates to MultiSend, the internal calls execute in the safe's context but do NOT pass through the safe's execTransaction flow, meaning the guard's checkTransaction is never invoked for those internal calls. The renter can batch transferFrom calls for all rented NFT

description.desc: if (operation == Enum.Operation.DelegateCall && !STORE.whitelistedDelegates(to)) revert — whitelisted delegates pass without further inspection of delegated logic
Affected function: toggleWhitelistDelegate

vulnerabilities.impact: CRITICAL if the configuration precondition is met. All rented NFTs in any rental safe would be stealable in a single transaction. The renter could also overwrite the guard's storage slot (slot keccak256('guard_manager.guard.address')) to permanently disable the guard. The empty checkAfterExecution means there is no safety net even for detecting such attacks post-execution.

vulnerabilities.title: CEI Violation in stopRent: Reentrancy via NFT Callback Before Storage Removal Enables Cross-Order Manipulation
vulnerabilities.summary: In stopRent, _reclaimRentedItems transfers NFTs back to the lender via safeTransferFrom, which triggers onERC721Received/onERC1155Received callbacks on the lender. At this point, the order is still active in Storage (orders[hash] == true) and rentalAssets haven't been decremented. A malicious lender contract can re-enter stopRent or stopRentBatch for a DIFFERENT order during this callback, potentially draining escrow funds or corrupting rental asset tracking. In stopRentBatch, the reentrancy win

description.desc: _reclaimRentedItems (external NFT transfer with callbacks) at line 226 before STORE.removeRentals at line 237; order still in storage during callback
Affected function: reclaimRentalOrder

vulnerabilities.impact: 1) DoS/Griefing: A malicious lender can prevent stopRentBatch from completing for any batch containing their orders, forcing each order to be stopped individually. 2) Stale state during reentrancy: During the callback, orders[hash] is still true and rentedAssets unchanged, which could have more severe consequences if protocol logic evolves. 3) The CEI violation is a structural code defect regardless of current exploitability. While direct fund theft was not identified in the current code, the reentrancy window with stale state is a latent vulnerability.

vulnerabilities.title: Immutable Domain Separator Enables Cross-Chain Signature Replay After Chain Fork
vulnerabilities.summary: The EIP-712 domain separator is computed at deployment as an immutable value using the deployment-time block.chainid. While _CHAIN_ID is stored, it is never compared against block.chainid at runtime in _recoverSignerFromPayload. After a chain fork (e.g., ETH/ETC-style split), the domain separator remains valid on both chains, enabling replay of rental creation signatures. An attacker can take a valid signature from one chain and replay it on the forked chain to create unwanted rentals.

description.desc: _DOMAIN_SEPARATOR.toTypedDataHash(payloadHash) always uses immutable separator; _CHAIN_ID stored but never checked against block.chainid

vulnerabilities.impact: The severity is HIGH for a fork scenario. After a fork: (1) any unexpired protocol signatures can be replayed on the forked chain, creating unauthorized rentals; (2) this could lock assets in rental safes on the forked chain; (3) the protocol signer cannot selectively invalidate signatures per-chain. Mitigating factors include: chain forks are rare events, signature expiration limits the replay window, and Seaport order nonces may provide additional replay protection for specific orders. Nevertheless, the code contains a clear defect - _CHAIN_ID is stored but never used, which is a textbook missing-check pattern.

vulnerabilities.title: Fee-on-Transfer Tokens Inflate Escrow Tracked Balance Causing Settlement Failures
vulnerabilities.summary: PaymentEscrow.increaseDeposit records the nominal amount from the order, but fee-on-transfer ERC20 tokens deliver less than the specified amount. This inflates balanceOf[token] relative to the actual token balance. When settlePayment attempts to transfer the full pre-fee amount, it may fail due to insufficient actual balance, permanently locking lender's NFTs because stopRent cannot complete.

description.desc: _increaseDeposit(token, amount) records nominal amount; no check that actual received matches

vulnerabilities.impact: HIGH impact. If a FOT token is used in a rental: (1) The specific rental becomes permanently unstoppable, locking lender NFTs. (2) The skim() function for that token permanently reverts due to underflow. (3) If multiple rentals use the same FOT token, earlier rentals may settle but later ones will fail as the cumulative deficit grows. (4) Recovery requires a contract upgrade of the PaymentEscrow module. The defect is a missing accounting safeguard - the code should measure actual received amounts rather than trusting nominal amounts.

vulnerabilities.title: State-dependent hooks can be manipulated to DoS stopRent, locking lender assets
vulnerabilities.summary: The stopRent function calls _removeHooks, which executes IHook(target).onStop(). If the hook reverts, the entire stopRent transaction reverts. A malicious renter can manipulate the external state that the hook depends on (e.g., a staking contract) to ensure the hook always reverts, permanently preventing the rental from being stopped and keeping the NFT in their Safe.

description.desc: try IHook(target).onStop() catches errors and explicitly reverts the transaction

vulnerabilities.impact: HIGH - Lender permanently loses access to their NFT, which remains locked in the renter's Safe. The renter effectively gets indefinite free use of the NFT. There is no emergency mechanism or admin override to recover the assets. The protocol has no way to force-stop a rental when hooks fail.

vulnerabilities.title: Hook Status Disabling Permanently Locks Rental Assets and Escrowed Payments
vulnerabilities.summary: When a rental is created with hooks that have onStop enabled, the hooks are recorded in the order. During stopRent, _removeHooks checks STORE.hookOnStop(target) and reverts if the hook's onStop bit is disabled. If a GUARD_ADMIN disables a hook's onStop status between rental creation and stop, all rentals referencing that hook become permanently un-stoppable. NFTs are locked in the rental Safe and ERC20 payments are locked in escrow indefinitely.

description.desc: _removeHooks reverts with Shared_DisabledHook if STORE.hookOnStop(target) returns false, with no fallback path
Affected function: hookOnStop

vulnerabilities.impact: HIGH severity. Any active rental using hooks whose onStop status is disabled becomes unstoppable. The lender loses access to their NFTs and escrowed payments until the admin re-enables the hook. If the hook was disabled due to being malicious/broken, re-enabling it to unblock stops re-exposes the protocol to the original risk. This is an architectural defect where admin operations can DoS user assets without any safeguard or emergency bypass.

vulnerabilities.title: Guard Reverts on Calldata Less Than 4 Bytes Trapping ETH in Rental Safes
vulnerabilities.summary: Guard.checkTransaction reverts if data.length < 4, blocking all transactions from the rental Safe that have empty or short calldata. This prevents the Safe owner from sending ETH via native transfers (which use empty calldata). Any ETH received by the rental Safe (e.g., from game rewards, refunds, or accidental sends) becomes permanently trapped.

description.desc: if (data.length < 4) { revert Errors.GuardPolicy_FunctionSelectorRequired(); }

vulnerabilities.impact: MEDIUM severity. The defect blocks a standard operation (native ETH transfer) from all rental safes permanently. While workarounds exist (dummy calldata to EOAs, WETH wrapping), these require technical knowledge that average users may not have. In gaming contexts where rental safes may frequently receive ETH rewards, this creates user friction and potential for confusion. The permanent nature (guard cannot be removed) amplifies the impact.

vulnerabilities.title: PAYEE Orders Have Zero On-Chain Footprint — Assets Unrecoverable by Protocol
vulnerabilities.summary: In Create._rentFromZone, when the order type is PAYEE, the entire storage recording block is skipped. No order hash is stored in Storage.orders, no rental asset updates are recorded, no ERC20 deposit increments occur, and no hooks are processed. PAYEE orders exist only as Seaport order events. If the paired PAY order fails, is never created, or is stopped independently, the PAYEE-side assets (NFTs and ERC20s transferred via Seaport considerations) have no protocol-level tracking and cannot be st

description.desc: if (payload.metadata.orderType.isBaseOrder() || payload.metadata.orderType.isPayOrder()) { ... } — PAYEE orders skip entire block containing addRentals, increaseDeposit, and _addHooks

vulnerabilities.impact: MEDIUM severity. The code defect is real: PAYEE orders produce zero on-chain state while still being validated by the zone. This violates INV-STATE-009 which requires all validated orders to be trackable. Specific impacts: (1) NFTs sent to a rental safe via PAYEE considerations are not tracked in rentedAssets, so the Guard will not protect them from transfer; (2) ERC20s sent to escrow are not tracked in balanceOf, appearing as skimmable surplus rather than escrowed funds; (3) No stopRent mechanism exists for PAYEE orders since they have no Storage.orders entry. Practical exploitability is LOW due to protocol signer gatekeeping and lack of economic incentive for standalone PAYEE fulfillment.
